i.question: What are the main changes that are introduced by The IRDA in health industry through the recent guidelines on health insurance?

Indicative Solution: IRDA has prescribed standard definition of main terms used in health insurance; for instance, deductible, hospital, pre-existing disease, congenital anomaly, subrogation, portability, etc. which all insurance companies need to follow to bring uniformity.
Standard definitions of major critical illness diseases have been provided by the regulator.
It has prescribed the disclosure that insurance companies need to incorporate in the prospectus and policy wording.
Regulator has also prescribed the declaration that prospect needs to sign in the proposal form.
Various forms such as cashless request and claim form, etc. have been standardized.

Premium Rates needs to be calculated based on the completed age of each policyholder as on the date of inception / renewal for individual policies. For FF policies it needs to be based on the incidence rates of all family members covered.
Loading / discount on renewal premiums can be based on the increase and decrease of premiums of the entire portfolio and not on individual claim experience.
Entry age of all health insurance products should be at least 65 years.
There shall not be any exit age beyond which renewal of health insurance policy is not available.
There should be a provision of free look period of 15 days in all products.
At least 50% of Cost of Pre Insurance Health Check Up should be borne by the insurance company in case the policy is accepted.

If the claim event falls between two policy periods, the claims shall be paid taking into consideration the available sum insured in both the policies and in case premiums of second year not yet paid, the same shall be adjusted against the claim amount and balance money paid to the policy holder.
Cumulative bonus in case of claim will be reduced at the same rate at which it is accrued.
Change in Premium and TPA needs to be informed to existing policy holders 3 months and 30 days in advance.
Insurance companies are required to set up a separate channel for addressing health insurance related claims and grievances of senior citizens.
Claims to be settled within 30 days of receipt of all documentation of claims.

If a person has purchased more than one indemnity policy from the same or different insurance companies, policyholder shall have the right to get his claim settled under any policy of his choice where the claim is below the sum assured limit, and in case the claim is above sum insured limit, contribution clause will be applicable.

IRDA has prescribed a comprehensive list of items that can be disallowed by the insurance company while processing claims. However, insurer may choose not to disallow some of the items from the list but may not disallow any item which is not mentioned there.
Appointed Actuary is supposed to review all the products at least once in a year and take corrective actions if any.

After five years of product approval Appointed Actuary shall review the performance of the product in terms of morbidity, lapse, inflation, expenses, etc. and compare with the original assumptions made while pricing the product and seek fresh approval with suitable justifications or modifications of the earlier assumptions made.

i.question: List down the key factors on which Family Floater (FF) discount depends?

Indicative Solution: Ages of the members
Annual Sum Insured limit
Number of members in the family
Type of product – indemnity or fixed benefit
Other pricing assumptions

ii.question: Explain the possible reasons which could result in the emergence of FF Discounts in Indian Context?

Indicative Solution: Positive selection or lack of anti-selection: Current portfolio may have shown experience of lower claim incidence rate in floater plans compared to similar individual plans. Possibility of existence of positive selection is expected to be higher at certain age bands and become more prominent after the PED waiting period is over. Selection effect depends on the product coverage: Lower for accident only products, Higher for indemnity products with comprehensive cover, Highest for CI only products which covers chronic diseases. The lower incidence rate effect can be passed on to premium rates as 'discount on Individual premium rate.'

Higher propensity to 'burn out' the sum insured (SI) limit: Need to check the factors and probability of an individual claim above SI Limit. Need to check the factors and probability of more than one member in a policy claim in the same policy year. The higher the chances of multiple claims in a year, higher should be the discounts. Need to check the overall probability of the total claim by all members in a family floater policy is more than SI limit.

Lower expense loading: Policy administration expenses – issuance, query handling, endorsements etc., and these costs are linked to number of policies. Claim handling expenses - these expenses per member may be lower due to lower claim incidence rate. Underwriting expenses - underwriting cost may be lower if the rejection ratio in floater policies is lower than in Individual policies.

Other Reasons: Better persistency, Customer life time value, Persistency increases as number of members in the family increase, Lesser frauds, Discretionary discounts to make floater a better proposition for customers

iii.question: Describe briefly the steps we will follow to arrive at the discounts due to claim costs savings due to Sum Insured Limit under a FF policy using stochastic modelling?

Indicative Solution: Simulate the gross claim amount for each member in the individual plan.
Calculate the net claim for each member for SI cap. This gives the pure claim cost for each member for given SI.
For family floater plan, simulate the gross claim amount for each member.
Summing the gross claim amounts for all members gives the gross claim amount for the family.
Get the net claim for the family by applying the SI cap. This gives the pure claim cost for floater SI.
Comparing the pure claim cost in step 3 with the total of step 7 gives the discount% applicable.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: You are a senior health actuary of a standalone health company, the product design and experience of the company are given below: Design on the Existing plan - Deductible of Rs 1,000/-, Co-pay = 30%, Out of Pocket Limit Rs 1,500 and thereafter 100% pay out by the company. Estimates for projected period from Jan to Dec of current year - Annual unleveraged trend = 15%, Density Function: Cumulative Frequency 0.6 Rs. 0, 0.75 Rs. 500, 0.85 Rs. 1,000, 0.90 Rs. 2,500, 0.95 Rs. 8,000, 1.00 Rs. 18,000

i.question: Calculate the annual leveraged trend for existing plan. [Hint: Annual Leverage Trend = Total Plan Cost after applying unleveraged trend / Total plan cost before applying unleveraged trend]

Indicative Solution: Member Cost calculation: Min(Annual Claim, Deductible Cost) + Max(Annual Claims Less: Deductible i.e. Rs 1,000/-, 0) x Co-payment (30%)
After applying unleveraged trend of 15%: Same calculation with inflated claims
Total Plan Cost before trend: Rs. 1,203
Total Plan Cost after trend: Rs. 1,424
Leveraged Trend = 1,424 / 1,203 = 118.4%

ii.question: What are the key differences between anti-selection and non-disclosure?

Indicative Solution: Anti-selection is the general effect of potential policyholders making use of information that is not available to the insurer, and so taking out cover on favourable terms. For example, people that think they are more likely to claim on an insurance contract tend to be the ones that take out those contracts. Anti-selection can be reasonable if, for example, the insurer does not ask for (or use) sufficient information required to accurately determine an applicant's risk of claiming.

Non-disclosure occurs in the specific circumstance where the applicant is asked to give the insurer details of information relevant to the risk, but fails to disclose such information. Deliberate non-disclosure may result in claims not being paid. Negligent non-disclosure may result in the benefit being reduced.

iii.question: Describe the purpose of investigating early claims, non-disclosure and anti-selective claims periodically as a specific monitoring exercise and possible ways in which its impact may be estimated or quantified.

Indicative Solution: At the onset of a claim, an investigation should be made as to the cause of the claim. This may be, for example, to spot any trends, e.g. epidemics, early so that action can be taken if necessary.
General monitoring at set intervals to see more gradual trends appearing, for example a decrease in claims costs due to medical advances.
The impact of anti-selection, non-disclosure and early claims is difficult to estimate. The actuary will first compare actual claims against those expected at early durations.

The actuary will next compare his office's experience with that of the market generally, where incidence data by duration is available; adjustment may be needed for differing policy conditions and target clientele.
Statistics from overseas or the population may be helpful, but their relevance needs to be examined and results treated with due caution.

On non-disclosure and anti-selection, the actuary will examine the questions asked at the underwriting stage and the particular methods employed by the distributor and compare these with practices elsewhere in the marketplace.

i.question: What is the meaning of the term 'Rating Factor' in the health insurance context?

Indicative Solution: Rating factor refers to the factor that affects premium for an insurance coverage.
In case of health insurance, rating factors are characteristics of an insured that determines the premium to be charged.
For example, in most of the health insurance policies premium is determined by age of insured, therefore, age can be termed as a rating factor.

ii.question: Describe the common rating factors prevalent in India for retail health inpatient indemnity product.

Indicative Solution: Age or Age-band: Age clearly has influence on both the frequency and severity of claim. In some of the products age is grouped in bands and premium remains same in a specific age band.
Sum Insured: Sum insured signifies the amount up to which the insurer will cover claims in a period of one year.
Location (Metro/ Non-metro): Although location is not widely used as a rating factor in India, some insurance companies do charge different rates based on residence of the insured.
Claim History: Some companies vary renewal premium based on the fact whether insured has claimed in the last policy year or not.

iii.question: Explain which are the potential rating factors that can be introduced in the above mentioned product?

Indicative Solution: Gender: Known to have impact of health care cost, can be explored as an additional rating factor.
Occupation/ Profession: Different occupations expose insured to different kind of risks.
Medical History: Can be used for premium determination.
Region: India is a vast country with varying climate and living conditions which have noteworthy impact on incidence of disease.
Habits: Both healthy and unhealthy habits can impact health care cost.
Socio economic status: Related to many aspects that can affect health care cost.

iv.question: What are the benefits of using more rating factors?

Indicative Solution: Using more rating factors results in dividing the whole health portfolio in finer groups. This leads to charging premium in line with the health care cost produced by various segment of insured. This is as per the Principle of Equity.

Not having enough number of rating factors leads to charging average premium to different sub groups. This assumes a specific business mix. If the business mix assumption does not fructify, it may result in different performance. This may lead to adverse selection and spiral effect.

v.question: What are the challenges in introducing these potential rating factors?

Indicative Solution: Data: There may not be sufficient statistical evidence to prove that the factors are actually risk factors.
Simplified Product: Many people advocate keeping the health insurance products simple.
Challenging Status quo: Companies do not want to take risk of introducing additional rating factors.
Regulatory Restrictions: Regulator may not allow some of the rating factors if these are considered discriminatory.
Difficult to monitor: It may be difficult to collect and process information around some rating factors.
Cost Implications: In case of some rating factors, the cost of collecting information may outweigh the benefits.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: You are the pricing actuary of an Insurance Company having large portfolio of health insurance products. You are in the process of revising rates of retail health inpatient indemnity product based on experience of loss years 2010, 2011 and 2012. The frequency and severity trend estimated by you are 2% p.a. and 8% p.a. respectively for this portfolio. Following are the details about the proposed filing: Probable date of filing: 1.4.2013, Estimated time taken by regulator to approve the product: 3 Months, Time taken to launch the product after approval: 3 Months, Minimum duration after which the premium of the product in question can be revised after its launch: 3 Years

i.question: Calculate the Trend Factors to be applied to historical data (loss year 2010 to 2012) to forecast losses for the proposed premium revision of the product in question assuming the policy duration is One year

Indicative Solution: Frequency Trend = 2%
Severity Trend = 8%
Combined Trend = 1.02 X 1.08 - 1 = 10.16%
For 2010 loss year: Trend Factor = 1.1016^5.25 = 1.66
For 2011 loss year: Trend Factor = 1.1016^4.25 = 1.51
For 2012 loss year: Trend Factor = 1.1016^3.25 = 1.37

ii.question: Calculate the Trend Factors to be applied to historical data (loss year 2010 to 2012) to forecast losses for the proposed premium revision of the product in question assuming the policy duration is Two years

Indicative Solution: Combined Trend = 10.16%
For 2010 loss year: Trend Factor = 1.1016^5.75 = 1.75
For 2011 loss year: Trend Factor = 1.1016^4.75 = 1.58
For 2012 loss year: Trend Factor = 1.1016^3.75 = 1.44

6.label: Question 6
6.scenario: XYZ General Insurance Company Ltd. is selling its health insurance products through Tied Agents, Brokers and Direct Channels in India. Its new Marketing Director who has taken charge recently is very excited about the potentials of Bancassurance as a channel to sell health insurance products.

i.question: Various segments of bank customers to whom XYZ may sell its health insurance products.

Indicative Solution: Saving Bank Account Holder: This is the major customer base for many banks in terms of count.
Credit Card Holder: Another customer base which can be huge in number.
Home Loan Customer: Generally, home loan customers have long term relationship with banks.
Other Loan Customer: Banks provide loans for various purposes.
Deposit and Investment: Some customers invest their surplus funds in bank deposit.
Current Account Holder: May vary from small entrepreneur to big corporate customers.

ii.question: Which insurance products can be sold through Bancassurance highlighting specific features of the proposed products?

Indicative Solution: Retail Inpatient Health Indemnity Product: Has a wide market and can be designed and customized for different customer segments.
Critical Illness: Relatively less premium and can be easily bundled with loans and credit card customers.
Group Health Insurance: Good selling proposition for holders of current accounts to cover their employees.
Personal Accident: Simple product with low premium.
Overseas Travel: Customers who approach bank for foreign exchange may be target buyer.
Long Term Care: Can be marketed to annuity holders and pensioners.

iii.question: Challenges that are specific to this sales channel.

Indicative Solution: Strong bargaining power: Banks are very big entities and much bigger than insurance companies.
Concentration of business: Overdependence on this channel may prove disastrous.
Meeting customer expectation: Insurance companies may be compelled to relent to pressure by banks to honour claims.
Promoter of own insurance company: In most cases banks have floated their own insurance companies.
Dual regulator: Banks regulated by RBI while insurance companies by IRDA.
Wide reach: Banks have wide reach but insurance companies may not have presence in remote areas.
